refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005 Refined: The NGHE solution shall automatically derive performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) from worksite mission context and operational parameters, record them in a traceable baseline, and maintain a managed history of all requirement updates and calibration changes with full audit trail.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on 'method' and lacked explicit traceability and history management. Refinement adds specificity: (1) automatic derivation from mission context, (2) explicit baseline recording, (3) managed history with audit trail. This aligns with acceptance criteria demanding documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained baselines/calibration updates. High safety criticality demands explicit safety margin derivation and audit capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verification shall confirm: (1) performance requirements are automatically computed from worksite mission inputs (e.g., terrain, payload, cycle count); (2) baseline is recorded with timestamp, source, and derivation method; (3) history log captures all updates with reason, timestamp, and approver; (4) traceability chain from worksite input → derived requirement → baseline → history is complete and auditable; (5) safety margins are explicitly calculated and documented.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) from Site Manager input.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirement set (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-driven algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record and persist performance baseline with full metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain managed history of all requirement and calibration updates with audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability reporting linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baseline → history.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized Performance Derivation Engine – A dedicated software module that ingests worksite mission context via API, executes requirement derivation algorithms (cycle time, energy, availability, safety margin calculations), persists baseline snapshots to a versioned configuration store, and maintains an immutable audit log of all changes.

DP-B: Distributed Derivation with Local Caching – Performance derivation logic embedded in Vehicle and Teleoperation modules independently, with periodic synchronization to a central history log; allows local optimization but risks inconsistency.

DP-C: Manual Requirement Entry with Post-Hoc Validation – Site Manager manually enters performance targets; system validates against mission context and records history; minimal automation, high human error risk.
DP-D: Hybrid Centralized + Operator Override – Centralized engine derives baseline; Operator/Tele-operator can override with justification; all overrides logged and flagged for safety review.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized Performance Derivation Engine with versioned baseline store and immutable audit log.

potential_sources: DP-A couples to worksite mission context input (FR-005.1) – requires well-defined API contract for mission data ingestion.
DP-A couples to requirement derivation algorithms (FR-005.2) – algorithms must be validated and certified for safety-critical performance (e.g., safety margin calculations).
DP-A couples to baseline persistence and history management (FR-005.3, FR-005.4) – requires reliable, auditable data store with version control semantics.
DP-A couples to traceability reporting (FR-005.5) – requires queryable audit log and lineage tracking.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A is decoupled from Vehicle and Teleoperation operational logic; it is a service layer that consumes mission context and produces requirement artifacts. This separation of concerns reduces coupling to downstream systems. However, the derivation algorithms themselves must be validated and certified, introducing a dependency on algorithm correctness and safety certification.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context is available in a structured, machine-readable format (e.g., JSON, protobuf) at system initialization or on-demand.
ASSUMPTION: Performance derivation algorithms (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margin calculations) are deterministic and can be validated/certified for safety-critical use.
ASSUMPTION: Baseline store and audit log can be implemented with sufficient reliability and performance to support real-time or near-real-time derivation.
RISK: If mission context is incomplete or ambiguous, derivation may produce invalid or unsafe performance targets; mitigation: validation rules and fallback to conservative defaults.
RISK: Algorithm changes or updates may invalidate historical baselines; mitigation: version algorithms and maintain lineage of algorithm versions in audit log.
RISK: Audit log growth may impact performance or storage; mitigation: implement log rotation, archival, and efficient querying.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-A is sound. Recommend adding explicit specification of: (1) mission context data schema and API contract; (2) derivation algorithm specifications and validation/certification approach; (3) baseline store schema and version control semantics; (4) audit log schema and retention policy. These specifications should be produced at the next decomposition level.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary. It owns the Performance Derivation Engine (DP-A) as a core service layer. The engine ingests mission context from Site Manager (via Tasking interface), executes derivation algorithms, and produces baseline and history artifacts. The engine is logically contained within NGHE Solution but may be physically realized as a distributed service (e.g., cloud-based or on-vehicle compute).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) flows from Site Manager into the Performance Derivation Engine via Tasking interface. This is the primary input to FR-005.1.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Derived performance requirements, baseline snapshots, and history metadata flow from the Performance Derivation Engine to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules via Telemetry interface. This supports operational awareness of performance targets and calibration state.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical performance margins and constraint violations are communicated to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface. This ensures safety-critical derivation results are visible to the Safety Manager.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Baseline store and audit log may be persisted to a remote repository (e.g., cloud backend) via Network Link. This supports long-term history management and cross-site traceability.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level boundary for the Performance Derivation Engine. The engine is a core system service that derives requirements for all downstream modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). Containment is high because the engine is logically cohesive and does not require distribution across multiple modules. Diffusion risk is minimal: the engine is a single logical service with well-defined inputs (Tasking) and outputs (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link).

Vehicle module is responsible for on-vehicle control and operation. Placing the Performance Derivation Engine in Vehicle would couple requirement derivation to vehicle-specific logic, reducing reusability and creating tight coupling. If derivation is in Vehicle, Teleoperation and Safety Services would need to query Vehicle for performance targets, creating diffusion and dependency inversion. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is responsible for remote operation and tele-operator interface. Placing the Performance Derivation Engine in Teleoperation would couple requirement derivation to tele-operator context, reducing applicability to autonomous or on-vehicle operation. Diffusion is high: Vehicle and Safety Services would depend on Teleoperation for performance targets. Not recommended.

Safety Services module is responsible for safety monitoring and enforcement. While safety margins are a critical output of the derivation engine, the engine itself is not a safety service—it is a requirement derivation service. Placing the engine in Safety Services would conflate requirement derivation with safety enforcement, reducing clarity and creating tight coupling. Safety Services should consume performance targets from the engine, not own it. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Performance Derivation Engine is a core system service that logically belongs in the NGHE Solution boundary. It is not specific to Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services; rather, it is a shared service that produces inputs for all downstream modules. Containment in NGHE Solution ensures: (1) single source of truth for performance requirement derivation; (2) clear separation of concerns (derivation vs. enforcement vs. operation); (3) reusability across multiple vehicles, tele-operators, and safety contexts; (4) centralized audit and traceability.
containment_risks: RISK: If the Performance Derivation Engine is implemented as a distributed service (e.g., cloud-based), there is a dependency on network connectivity and cloud availability. Mitigation: implement local caching and fallback to cached baselines if cloud is unavailable.
RISK: If the engine is on-vehicle, there is a risk of inconsistency between on-vehicle and cloud baselines. Mitigation: implement synchronization protocol and conflict resolution.
RISK: If the engine is in a separate microservice, there is a risk of latency in requirement derivation. Mitigation: implement caching and pre-computation of common scenarios.
RISK: Audit log growth may impact performance. Mitigation: implement log rotation, archival, and efficient querying.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: IM assignment to NGHE Solution is sound. Recommend clarifying the physical realization of the Performance Derivation Engine (on-vehicle, cloud-based, or hybrid) at the next decomposition level. This will inform interface design, latency requirements, and fault tolerance strategies.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (Centralized Performance Derivation Engine) directly addresses all sub-FRs: (1) ingests mission context via API (FR-005.1); (2) executes derivation algorithms (FR-005.2); (3) records baseline with metadata (FR-005.3); (4) maintains audit log (FR-005.4); (5) supports traceability reporting (FR-005.5). The DP is complete, specific, and verifiable. No coupling issues identified. DP-A is the recommended solution.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: If the Performance Derivation Engine is implemented as a distributed service, there is a dependency on network connectivity. Mitigation: implement local caching and fallback mechanisms.
DIFFUSION RISK: If the engine is on-vehicle, there is a risk of inconsistency with cloud baselines. Mitigation: implement synchronization and conflict resolution.
DIFFUSION RISK: If the engine is in a separate microservice, there is a risk of latency. Mitigation: implement caching and pre-computation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary. The Performance Derivation Engine is a core system service that should not be distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. Containment score of 95 for NGHE Solution reflects high cohesion and low diffusion. The engine is a single logical service with well-defined inputs and outputs.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-005 is feasible. Test cases can be designed to: (1) ingest mission context and verify derivation correctness; (2) record baseline and verify metadata; (3) update requirements and verify audit log; (4) query traceability and verify lineage. Acceptance criteria (documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs, maintained baselines/calibration updates) are directly verifiable against the NGHE Solution module.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns the Performance Derivation Engine and is responsible for delivering CR-NGHE-005. The engine consumes Tasking (mission context) and produces Telemetry (performance targets), SafetyAlert (safety margins), and Network Link (history persistence). All interfaces are known and well-defined.

recommended_revisions: Clarify the physical realization of the Performance Derivation Engine (on-vehicle, cloud-based, or hybrid) at the next decomposition level.
Specify the mission context data schema and API contract for Tasking interface.
Specify the derivation algorithm specifications and validation/certification approach.
Specify the baseline store schema and version control semantics.
Specify the audit log schema and retention policy.
Define fallback and caching strategies for network unavailability.
Define synchronization and conflict resolution for distributed scenarios.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-005 has been successfully refined, a sound DP has been selected, and a correct IM assignment has been made. The refined FR is not atomic—it contains five sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.5) that require further decomposition. The next level should decompose each sub-FR into its own FR-DP-IM chain. All gates have passed. Proceed to decompose sub-FRs.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) from Site Manager input.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirement set (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-driven algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record and persist performance baseline with full metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain managed history of all requirement and calibration updates with audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability reporting linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baseline → history.

assumptions: Worksite mission context is available in a structured, machine-readable format at system initialization or on-demand.
Performance derivation algorithms are deterministic and can be validated/certified for safety-critical use.
Baseline store and audit log can be implemented with sufficient reliability and performance.
Network connectivity is available for cloud-based persistence (with local fallback).
Site Manager provides mission context via Tasking interface in a timely manner.
open_risks: Incomplete or ambiguous mission context may produce invalid performance targets; mitigation: validation rules and conservative defaults.
Algorithm changes may invalidate historical baselines; mitigation: version algorithms and maintain lineage.
Audit log growth may impact performance; mitigation: log rotation and archival.
Network unavailability may prevent baseline persistence; mitigation: local caching and synchronization.
Inconsistency between on-vehicle and cloud baselines in distributed scenarios; mitigation: synchronization and conflict resolution.
evidence_needed: Mission context data schema and API contract specification.
Derivation algorithm specifications and validation/certification evidence.
Baseline store schema and version control design.
Audit log schema and retention policy.
Synchronization and conflict resolution protocol (if distributed).
Test cases for derivation correctness, baseline recording, history management, and traceability.
Acceptance test plan demonstrating documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained baselines/calibration updates.
